text: The surgeon initiates dissection by opening the peritoneal leaf of the transverse mesocolon, starting medially towards the triads.
The surgeon dissects the mesocolon from the body and tail of the pancreas, facilitated by the absence of the left branch of the middle colic artery, and opens the epiploon retrocavity.

The surgeon performs vascular dissection, revealing the left colic vessels. These vessels are sealed using a radiofrequency device at the confluence with the inferior mesenteric vein, without the use of clips or other suture devices.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the left retrocolic space along Toldt's fascia, progressing from a medial to lateral direction.
The surgeon separates the transverse mesocolon from the triads, facilitating the mobilization of the medial transverse colon in preparation for anastomosis.
The surgeon performs cleavage of the left colon from the left peritoneal colic, progressing from the bottom to the top over the splenic flexure.
The surgeon uses a linear stapler to section the descending and transverse colon, completing the mesocolon dissection.

Following the resection of the specimen, the surgeon performs a primary anastomosis using a stapler to create a completely intracorporeal side-to-side isoperistaltic connection between the transverse and descending colon.
